title: Positive Pairs
learningObjectives: Define what a positive pair is and how it encodes a proximity criterion
Explain at least four strategies for constructing positive pairs
Identify what information is preserved and what is discarded by each strategy
Predict how positive pair construction affects learned representation structure

whyThisLesson.text: The positive pair is the heart of contrastive learning — it is the operational definition of what 'similar' means. Every choice about how to construct positives embeds an assumption about which variation is informative and which is noise. Understanding positive pair construction is essential before studying the loss functions that act on them.

sections.bridgeFrom: In the previous lesson we established that the proximity criterion is a design decision. A positive pair is the concrete implementation of that criterion: two inputs that the model is trained to represent similarly.

intuition.analogy: Consider teaching a child what makes two objects 'the same kind'. You show them pairs: 'these two are the same kind.' If you pair objects by colour, the child learns that colour defines kind. If you pair objects by function (both are for cutting), the child learns functional identity. The child never sees your rule — only the pairs.
intuition.analogyLimit: A child can notice and question your pairing rule; a neural network cannot — it will faithfully learn whatever latent structure the positive pairs imply, even if that structure is spurious or uninformative for the intended task.
intuition.text: In practice, positive pairs are constructed by applying two different transformations to the same input, or by grouping inputs that share a known label or relationship. The key insight is that whatever is stable across the positive pair will be retained in the representation, and whatever varies will be compressed away. Positive pair design is thus a form of inductive bias injection: you are telling the model what to ignore.

toyExample.description: 6 sentences: 'The cat sat', 'A feline rested', 'The dog ran', 'A canine sprinted', 'The cat ate', 'The dog barked'.

steps.action: Construct positive pairs by paraphrase: ('The cat sat', 'A feline rested')
steps.result: Model learns that 'cat' and 'feline' refer to the same entity; representations encode semantic content, not syntax
steps.insight: Semantic paraphrase pairs encourage semantic encoding

steps.action: Construct positive pairs by subject: all sentences about cats are positive
steps.result: ('The cat sat', 'A feline rested', 'The cat ate') all cluster; 'cat' and 'feline' map close; actions vary within cluster
steps.insight: Subject-based pairs encourage subject identity encoding; predicate information is less reliable

steps.action: Construct positive pairs by random sentence dropout augmentation: ('The cat sat', 'cat sat'), ('The dog ran', 'dog')
steps.result: Model learns which words are redundant (articles, some verbs) and which are essential
steps.insight: Augmentation-based positive pairs encode what survives the augmentation

visual.question: How does the angle between two representations change as the encoder is trained on different positive pair strategies?
visual.description: Adjust two vector representations and observe how cosine similarity changes; use this to build intuition about what 'close' means geometrically before loss functions are applied

technical.text: Positive pairs (x, x⁺) are drawn from a distribution p_pos(x, x⁺) that encodes the proximity criterion. Four principal construction strategies: (1) Data augmentation: x⁺ = aug(x) where aug is a stochastic transformation. (2) Multi-view: x and x⁺ are different modalities or views of the same underlying entity (image and caption). (3) Label-based: x and x⁺ share the same class or attribute label. (4) Co-occurrence: x and x⁺ appear together in context (adjacent sentences, co-occurring words). Each strategy makes different assumptions about what constitutes 'sameness'. The strategy also determines which negative pairs are valid — anything that is not a positive for a given anchor.

definitions.definition: A pair of inputs (x, x⁺) that the contrastive objective trains the encoder to map to nearby representations; operationalises the proximity criterion
A probability distribution over input transformations used to generate positive pairs; determines which input variation is treated as noise
A positive pair constructed from two different representations or modalities of the same underlying entity, such as an image and its textual description

mathematics.label: Positive pair sampling distribution
mathematics.latex: (x, x^+) \sim p_{\text{pos}}(x, x^+) = p(x) \cdot T(x^+ | x)

symbols.T(x^+ | x): conditional distribution of positives given anchor; encodes the proximity criterion

mathematics.plainExplanation: A positive pair is drawn by first sampling an anchor x, then sampling a positive x⁺ from the conditional distribution T which encodes the chosen proximity criterion.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Makes the proximity criterion explicit and probabilistic, enabling analysis of what information the contrastive objective can and cannot recover
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without a specified T, the notion of 'positive pair' is undefined and the contrastive objective cannot be computed
mathematics.encourages: Representations that are invariant to variation within T(x⁺ | x)
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the invariance encoded by T is semantically meaningful or useful for downstream tasks

methodComparison.question: What information does each positive pair strategy preserve versus discard?

methods.name: Augmentation-based positive pair
methods.answer: Preserves: features stable under the chosen augmentations (e.g., object identity under colour jitter). Discards: features that vary with augmentation (colour, texture, spatial position if using random crops).

methods.name: Label-based positive pair
methods.answer: Preserves: features correlated with the label (e.g., class-defining features). Discards: within-class variation. Risk: if the label is coarse, fine-grained distinctions within class are suppressed.

methods.name: Co-occurrence-based positive pair
methods.answer: Preserves: features predictive of co-occurrence (context, topic). Discards: features unique to individual occurrences. Risk: co-occurrence may encode spurious correlations rather than semantic content.

methodComparison.practicalImplication: The positive pair strategy determines the information bottleneck; choose it by asking 'what do I want the representation to ignore?'

evidence.claim: Random cropping combined with colour jitter is the most critical augmentation combination for SimCLR performance on ImageNet
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.section: Section 3, Ablation study on data augmentation
evidence.paraphrase: Chen et al. found that composing random cropping (which forces the model to match different views of the same object) with colour jitter (which prevents colour histogram shortcuts) was critical; either alone was substantially weaker, indicating the positive pair strategy must jointly handle multiple potential shortcuts

failureModes.name: Shortcut positive pairs
failureModes.mechanism: Positive pairs share a superficial feature (e.g., same photographer watermark) that is easier to detect than the intended semantic feature
failureModes.symptom: Low contrastive loss but the encoder uses the shortcut feature rather than the intended feature; downstream task performance is poor
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Evaluate the encoder on a dataset where the shortcut feature has been removed; performance collapse indicates reliance on the shortcut
failureModes.mitigation: Design augmentations that remove the shortcut feature, or collect positive pairs that explicitly lack the shortcut
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: If SetConCA's set-based positive pairs share a spurious feature (e.g., all tokens in a set are from the same syntactic construction), the encoder may learn syntactic rather than semantic representations

setconcaConnection.relevance: SetConCA constructs positive pairs from tokens within the same semantically coherent set; the quality of this set construction directly determines what the contrastive objective encourages
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The semantic set construction step of SetConCA
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: How to define 'same set': by co-reference, by topic, by task context, or by learned clustering
setconcaConnection.whichFailureExplained: If set boundaries do not align with semantic boundaries, positive pairs may share only surface features, and the learned representations will reflect surface rather than semantic structure
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Vary the set construction strategy (topic model, co-reference resolution, random grouping) and compare the semantic content of the resulting representations using probing classifiers
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: Even with perfect semantic sets, the contrastive objective may encourage representations that are useful for retrieval within the corpus but not for the interpretability tasks SetConCA targets

exercises.prompt: You train a contrastive model where positive pairs are two sentences from the same Wikipedia article. Predict what features the representation will encode well and what it will encode poorly.
exercises.reveal: The model will encode topic well (same article → same topic), and may encode domain or style. It will encode individual facts or specific claims poorly, because two sentences from a long article may differ substantially in their specific content. Named entities may be encoded inconsistently — 'Napoleon' and 'the Emperor' in the same article may or may not become close depending on coreference structure.

exercises.prompt: A contrastive model trained on medical imaging shows excellent performance on chest X-ray disease classification but near-random performance on pathology slide classification. The positive pairs were constructed from augmented chest X-ray images. Diagnose the problem.
exercises.reveal: The positive pair strategy (augmentations designed for chest X-rays) encoded features specific to X-ray imaging (texture, density) and not features generalisable to pathology slides. The proximity criterion was not domain-agnostic. The model learned a domain-specific proximity criterion, not a general medical imaging one.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Design a positive pair construction strategy for learning representations of natural language instructions that would make the representations useful for both retrieval (find similar instructions) and execution (predict the action sequence). Identify the inherent tension and how you would resolve it.
rubric: Identifies that retrieval requires surface/paraphrase similarity while execution requires functional equivalence
Notes that augmentations preserving surface form help retrieval but may not help execution
Proposes a strategy (e.g., paraphrase pairs for retrieval, same action sequence pairs for execution)
Discusses multi-task or multi-objective positive pair construction
Acknowledges that a single proximity criterion may not satisfy both goals simultaneously
masteryQuestions.badVersion: Use data augmentation to create positive pairs.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: For retrieval, I would use paraphrase pairs (semantically equivalent instructions with different surface forms) to encode meaning independent of wording. For execution, I would use pairs of instructions that map to the same action sequence (even if worded differently). These two criteria conflict: 'Turn left then go straight' and 'Go right then reverse' may have similar surface forms but opposite execution sequences. I would resolve this with a multi-head approach: one head trained with paraphrase positives for retrieval, another with action-equivalence positives for execution, with a shared trunk encoding common features.

researchWorksheet.claim: The positive pair construction strategy is the primary determinant of what features contrastive models encode
researchWorksheet.requiredAssumptions: Encoder capacity is not the bottleneck; the optimiser converges; negative pairs are sampled from the data marginal
researchWorksheet.evidenceProvided: SimCLR ablations show augmentation strategy qualitatively changes encoded features; word2vec context window determines encoded semantic relationships
researchWorksheet.strongestAlternativeExplanation: Architecture inductive biases and the distribution of negative pairs may jointly determine feature encoding, with positive pair strategy as one factor among several
researchWorksheet.missingControl: A study systematically varying positive pair strategy while holding negatives, architecture, and dataset fixed across multiple downstream tasks
researchWorksheet.closestBaseline: SimCLR with varying augmentation policies evaluated on multiple downstream tasks
researchWorksheet.falsifyingResult: If two models with different positive pair strategies but identical negatives and architectures learn representations that are linearly equivalent on all tasks, positive pair strategy would not be the primary determinant
researchWorksheet.setconcaImplication: SetConCA should carefully audit what features its semantic sets share, and verify that these features correspond to the interpretability-relevant structure it aims to capture
researchWorksheet.proposedExperiment: Train 6 encoders with identical architectures, each with a different positive pair strategy, on the same corpus. Evaluate each on 8 downstream tasks spanning retrieval, classification, generation, and interpretability. Compute a task-pair correlation matrix to identify which strategies generalise across task types.

bridgeToNext.text: Positive pairs define what should be close; negative pairs define what should be far. The next lesson examines how negatives are chosen, why this choice is as consequential as positive pair design, and how the balance between positives and negatives shapes the learned geometry.
